subject: Chemistry
title: Scheele's Green: Arsenic Wallpaper
description: One-minute skill: Scheele's Green: Arsenic Wallpaper.

hook.text: In the 1800s, green wallpaper was slowly killing families. The pigment was copper arsenite—laced with arsenic.

buildup.text: Scheele's Green was a vivid, cheap pigment made by reacting copper sulfate with arsenic trioxide.

discovery.text: In damp conditions, mold on the wallpaper converted the arsenic into toxic gas. Residents inhaled poison nightly.

twist.text: Napoleon may have been slowly poisoned by his green wallpaper on St. Helena. Hair samples show high arsenic levels.

climax.text: Even after the danger was known, green arsenic paint was used on children's toys and candy wrappers for decades.

punchline.text: A beautiful green pigment that released arsenic gas. Fashion literally killed.

quiz.question: How did arsenic green wallpaper poison people?
options: Mold on damp wallpaper converted arsenic into toxic gas that was inhaled
The green color itself damaged people's eyesight
Arsenic leaked through walls and contaminated drinking water
